title: Merchandise exports by continent of destination
subtitle: Figures correspond to the value of [merchandise exports](#dod:merchandise-exports) by continental destination as a share of [gross domestic product](#dod:gdp) (GDP). All partner countries are classified into continent groupings according to OWID's classification.
caption:
Merchandise exports by continent of destination

Figures correspond to the value of merchandise exports by continental destination as a share of gross domestic product (GDP). All partner countries are classified into continent groupings according to OWID's classification.

This stacked area chart tracks India's merchandise exports by continent of destination from 1949 to 2014, expressed as a percentage of gross domestic product. The vertical axis ranges from 0% to 18%, while the horizontal axis marks years in ten-year increments. The data is divided into six colored bands representing exports to Asia, Western Europe, North America, South America, Africa, Eastern Europe, and Oceania. Exports to Asia, shown in blue at the base of the stack, begin near 2% in 1949, dip below 2% around 1970, and rise steadily after 1990 to reach approximately 8% by 2014. The maroon band for Western Europe sits above Asia, starting around 2% in 1949, fluctuating between 1% and 3% through the 1980s, then expanding to roughly 3-4% by the end of the period. The green band for North America remains relatively thin throughout, generally staying under 2%. The orange band for South America, yellow for Africa, purple for Eastern Europe, and pink for Oceania occupy the upper layers of the stack, each contributing smaller shares that rarely exceed 1-2% individually. The total height of all stacked areas reaches its peak around 2008, briefly exceeding 18%, before settling near 16% in 2014. These estimates come from dyadic transactions data, where series like "Western Europe" represent the sum of exports between all Western European countries divided by the GDP of Western Europe.
